ISAAC MASTER ARCHITECTURE / ANALYSIS / CONTEXT FILE

This file was created to consolidate:
1. the four external model analyses provided in this chat
2. the intended Isaac target architecture and concept
3. the Evolution 2.0 understanding developed in this chat
4. the practical work-package ordering agreed in this chat
5. the key module source files from the current repository snapshot

Purpose of this file
This file is meant to be given to strong architecture or coding models so they can work from one consolidated source of truth.

Important instruction for models reading this file
- There is no time limit.
- Optimize for correctness, structure, architectural clarity, and future extensibility.
- Do not give generic chatbot advice.
- Do not reduce Isaac to a wrapper or ordinary assistant.
- Isaac is intended to become a local, stateful, evolving AI cognitive kernel.
- Human planning and architecture authority remain primary.
- Implementation agents should follow scoped work packages, not uncontrolled rewrites.

Section overview
A. External model analyses
B. Isaac target architecture / concept / Evolution 2.0
C. Additional distilled knowledge from this chat
D. Source module appendices from repository snapshot


====================================================================================================
A. EXTERNAL MODEL ANALYSES
====================================================================================================

A1. GROQ ANALYSIS 1
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. CORE FLOW SUMMARY
The current end-to-end runtime flow (visible strictly inside the five modules) is:
User input → isaac_core.IsaacKernel.process
→ immediate low_complexity.classify_interaction (single source of truth for trivial routing)
→ if is_lightweight_local_class → local_fast_response and short-circuit (no further orchestration)
→ otherwise: intent detection (local PATTERNS in kernel) + _retrieve_relevant_context (facts, directives, task_results, working memory from memory) → _select_response_strategy (based on classification + retrieval) → build enriched prompt (memory.build_context + structured retrieval) → executor.Executor.create_task + submit_and_wait
→ Executor worker dequeues → _execute (type-specific) → for CHAT/AI: _execute_ai loop that re-classifies (low_complexity), optionally calls tools, asks external provider, then logic.evaluate + logic.decide_followup → possible decompose/rephrase/switch loop inside executor → final answer
→ back to kernel → memory.add_conversation (both sides) + memory.save_task_result → _post_process (score-based side effects) → output.
Classification happens first and is consulted again inside executor; retrieval happens before strategy selection; execution lives inside the task loop; persistence is scattered.

2. MODULE-BY-MODULE ANALYSIS
isaac_core.py (IsaacKernel)
• Current role: central orchestrator and process entry point.
• What it currently owns: full input-to-output pipeline, intent detection (PATTERNS), lightweight short-circuit, retrieval-before-strategy logic (_retrieve_relevant_context, _select_response_strategy, _format_retrieval_context), task creation, memory conversation logging, post-process side-effects.
• What it should own: high-level routing, classification-driven dispatch, pre-execution context assembly, strategy selection.
• What it should NOT own: low-level task queuing/execution loops, quality evaluation, or direct persistence calls (these leak into it).
• How it relates to the others: creates and submits Task objects to executor; calls low_complexity for first routing decision; calls memory for retrieval and later persistence; never calls logic directly (delegates via executor).
executor.py (Executor + Task)
• Current role: task engine and heavy execution orchestrator (the largest file).
• What it currently owns: task lifecycle (queue, worker, status machine), _execute_ai iteration loop, tool decision (_should_try_tool which re-uses low_complexity), followup loop (calls logic), code/file/search special paths, persistence of task_results.
• What it should own: deterministic execution of a pre-defined Task (including iteration limits and provider fallback), nothing more.
• What it should NOT own: re-classification logic, tool strategy decisions, quality scoring, followup decision logic, or any intent re-interpretation.
• How it relates to the others: receives Task (with interaction_class and strategy flags) from kernel; calls low_complexity again internally; calls logic.evaluate/decide_followup; calls memory.save_task_result and loads persisted tasks.
low_complexity.py
• Current role: fast, deterministic interaction classifier + trivial-response generator.
• What it currently owns: InteractionClass taxonomy, keyword+word-count classification, lightweight-local fast path, normalize function.
• What it should own: classification as the single source of truth for all routing decisions (trivial vs normal vs tool vs status).
• What it should NOT own: response generation for anything beyond the three lightweight classes (local_class_response should stay narrow).
• How it relates to the others: used first by kernel for routing short-circuit; re-used by executor for tool gating and lightweight checks inside AI loop.
logic.py
• Current role: quality gate and followup oracle.
• What it currently owns: QualityScore calculation, evasion/vagueness detection, TopicExtractor, FollowUpDecision (rephrase/targeted/decompose/switch), evaluation weights from config.
• What it should own: post-execution quality scoring and justified followup decisions only.
• What it should NOT own: topic extraction for routing (currently mixed with classification), or any execution decisions.
• How it relates to the others: called exclusively from inside executor._execute_ai; never called directly by kernel or memory.
memory.py (Memory)
• Current role: typed SQLite persistence layer with working memory.
• What it currently owns: conversations table + FTS, facts table, directives table, task_results table, build_context, search_facts, set_fact, add_conversation, working-memory RAM cache.
• What it should own: all typed storage, retrieval APIs (facts, directives, task_results, conversations), context assembly for the kernel.
• What it should NOT own: direct calls from executor for task_results (should be mediated), or vector memory (not in these files yet).
• How it relates to the others: kernel uses it for pre-strategy retrieval and post-execution logging; executor uses it only for save_task_result and loading persisted tasks.

3. CURRENT CONNECTION MAP
Control flow: kernel.process → low_complexity.classify → (if not lightweight) kernel._route → kernel._standard_task → executor.create_task/submit → executor worker → (for AI) executor._execute_ai → logic.evaluate/decide_followup (loop) → memory persistence.
Data flow: classification string is passed down into Task and re-inspected by executor; retrieval dicts (facts/directives/task_results) flow from memory → kernel strategy → enriched prompt → Task → executor (but not re-used inside execution).
Context flow: memory.build_context + custom _retrieve_relevant_context → kernel → prompt → Task; task_results are written by both kernel and executor; working memory is loaded once at Memory init and used by both.

4. MISSING OR WEAK LINKS
• Classification is consulted twice (kernel + executor) with no single source-of-truth contract → implicit routing divergence.
• Retrieval happens before strategy (good) but the retrieval interface in kernel is ad-hoc (_retrieve_relevant_context + manual memory calls) instead of a typed Memory API.
• Executor silently re-interprets intent via _should_try_tool and re-classifies instead of respecting the Task’s interaction_class and strategy flags.
• No explicit “strategy object” passed down; flags (allow_tools, allow_followup, etc.) are set in kernel but partially ignored/overridden inside executor.
• Memory persistence of conversations/task_results is duplicated (kernel does add_conversation + save_task_result; executor also calls save_task_result).
• No typed retrieval-before-response contract enforced; kernel and executor both reach into memory independently.
• Learning/feedback loop is implicit (only score is stored); no auditable “outcome → memory update” step inside these modules.
These cause the instability symptoms visible in the code: duplicate classification, tool calls on normal chat, followup loops that ignore the original classification, and scattered persistence.

5. TARGET CONNECTION MAP FOR THESE 5 MODULES
Classification (low_complexity) must be the single entry gate in kernel.process and the only place that decides lightweight short-circuit or TaskType/strategy. The resulting InteractionClass + strategy dict must be immutable inside the Task and respected by executor (no re-classify).
Retrieval (memory) → must happen strictly before strategy selection inside kernel (already almost true). Memory must expose clean typed retrieval methods (e.g. get_typed_context_for_strategy) that kernel calls once; executor never calls memory directly except through the Task’s pre-fetched retrieved_context.
Strategy selection (kernel._select_response_strategy) → produces an immutable Strategy object that is attached to the Task. Executor receives the Task and executes exactly according to the strategy flags and interaction_class (no reinterpretation).
Executor boundaries: executor only executes what the Task already declares (type + strategy + pre-fetched context). All quality evaluation and followup decisions stay inside executor but are driven purely by the pre-set Task parameters and logic module.
Memory usage: kernel uses memory for pre-response retrieval and post-response structured logging (add_conversation + save_task_result). Executor only writes task_results through the Task (no direct memory calls). All context flows through kernel → Task → executor.
This creates a clean pipeline: classify → retrieve → strategy → task → execute(deterministically) → evaluate(with logic) → store(typed).

6. IMMEDIATE ARCHITECTURAL PRIORITIES
Stabilize first (within these 5 modules only):
1. Make low_complexity.classify_interaction the single source of truth: remove the second classify call inside executor._should_try_tool and replace with Task.interaction_class check.
2. Extract a small immutable Strategy dataclass in kernel and pass it explicitly to Task.
3. Introduce typed retrieval methods on Memory so kernel no longer does ad-hoc searches.
4. Remove direct memory.save_task_result from executor; let kernel handle all persistence after task completion.
Add next:
• A small ContextAssembler inside kernel that owns the “retrieval-before-response” contract.
• Clear executor boundary: executor must not call classify or re-decide tool strategy.
Do NOT build yet: any new learning/feedback mechanisms, vector memory extensions, or new TaskTypes beyond what is already in executor.

7. RISKS
If the current structure is not fixed:
• Executor will keep growing into a god-class that silently overrides kernel intent (already happening with tool gating and re-classification).
• Routing will diverge between trivial classification path and normal-chat path, violating the “classification must control routing” principle.
• Strategy selection will remain implicit and brittle, leading to normal chat occasionally triggering tools or followups that should have been short-circuited.
• Persistence will stay duplicated and untyped, making gradual learning impossible to audit.
• The system will never achieve deterministic execution boundaries, so “evolve over time without losing architectural clarity” becomes impossible; every new feature will add more implicit cross-module coupling.


A2. GROQ ANALYSIS 2
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Exploration of Modular Design Patterns for Isaac’s Cognitive Kernel
Isaac’s target architecture (classification-first routing, strict retrieval-before-response, deterministic executor boundaries, typed memory, lightweight short-circuiting, and gradual auditable evolution) aligns strongly with established modular patterns from modern AI agent and cognitive system literature. Below is a focused exploration of the patterns that directly address the weak links identified in the first package analysis (e.g., duplicated classification, implicit strategy, executor overreach, scattered persistence). I draw only from patterns that map to the five core modules (isaac_core.py, executor.py, low_complexity.py, logic.py, memory.py) and the stated principles—no unrelated or future-module speculation.
1. Microkernel / Plugin Architecture (Primary Fit)
The microkernel pattern consists of a minimal core system that manages only essential orchestration (communication, routing, basic lifecycle) while all application-specific behavior lives in independent, pluggable modules that register via well-defined interfaces.
• Core responsibilities: classification, routing decisions, retrieval orchestration, strategy selection, and final persistence.
• Plugins/modules: specialized executors, evaluators, typed stores, and future extensions (e.g., tool runners) that the kernel loads dynamically but never re-implements.
Why it solves Isaac’s current issues:
• Low-complexity classification becomes the sole gatekeeper plugin that the kernel registers first—eliminating the second classify call inside executor.
• Executor becomes a pure execution plugin that receives an immutable Task + Strategy contract; it cannot reinterpret intent or call classify again.
• Memory becomes a typed repository plugin; kernel asks it for pre-strategy context once.
• Evolution stays clean: new capabilities (e.g., advanced followup modes) are added as plugins without touching the kernel.
This directly enforces “classification must control routing” and “executor must execute, not silently redefine intent.”
2. Hexagonal (Ports & Adapters) + Clean Architecture (Layering on the Kernel)
Hexagonal architecture isolates the domain core (IsaacKernel logic) from infrastructure via ports (interfaces) and adapters (implementations). Clean Architecture adds concentric layers: entities → use-cases → interfaces → frameworks/drivers.
• Domain core: classification → retrieval → strategy selection → task creation (pure functions in isaac_core).
• Ports: e.g., MemoryPort (get_typed_context_for_strategy, persist_outcome), ExecutorPort (execute_task), LogicPort (evaluate_and_decide_followup).
• Adapters: current SQLite memory, executor worker loop, logic evaluator.
Mapping to current modules:
• Kernel owns the domain use-case “ProcessInput”.
• Memory, executor, and logic become adapters behind ports.
• This prevents the current leakage where executor calls memory.save_task_result directly and kernel reaches into memory for ad-hoc queries.
It guarantees “retrieval must happen before response strategy selection” and keeps type separation and identity boundaries intact.
3. Modular Monolith (Deployment + Organization Pattern)
A modular monolith organizes code into feature-scoped modules/packages that are independently developable and testable but still deployed as a single process/binary. This is the practical middle ground between a pure monolith and microservices—especially valuable for a local, stateful system.
• Modules in Isaac would be: core.kernel, core.classifier, core.memory, core.logic, core.executor.
• Each module exposes only its public API (no cross-module direct imports of internals).
Direct benefit for the five files:
• isaac_core.py stays small and focused on orchestration.
• executor.py shrinks dramatically once it becomes just the ExecutorPort adapter.
• Memory remains the single typed store without duplication.
This pattern preserves architectural clarity during evolution and avoids the fragility of full distribution while allowing independent evolution of classification, memory, or logic.
4. Typed Repository + Retrieval-Before-Response Pattern (Memory-Specific)
Memory is treated as a typed repository with explicit query contracts rather than a generic store. Retrieval is a mandatory, orchestrated step before any strategy or execution decision—not an opportunistic call inside the loop.
• Memory module exposes typed methods (e.g. get_context_for_strategy(interaction_class, query) that returns a structured Context object).
• Kernel calls this exactly once per normal input.
• Executor receives the pre-fetched context inside the immutable Task and never queries memory again.
This eliminates the current ad-hoc _retrieve_relevant_context + manual memory calls in kernel and the duplicate persistence calls.
5. Strategy + Compositional Hybrid Patterns (For Logic and Followup)
Logic module implements the Strategy pattern (immutable Strategy object attached to Task) combined with hybrid compositional patterns (elementary building blocks like evaluate + decide_followup that can be chained without side effects).
• Kernel selects the strategy once (after retrieval).
• Logic plugin receives the strategy + answer + score and returns a FollowUpDecision that executor must obey exactly.
• No implicit topic extraction or re-decision inside executor.
This enforces “normal chat must not opportunistically trigger tools” and makes followup deterministic and justified.
Recommended Application Order to the Current Five Modules
1. Stabilize the microkernel core in isaac_core.py: introduce explicit ports/interfaces and make low_complexity the registered classifier plugin.
2. Extract Strategy dataclass and enforce it on every Task (kernel → executor contract).
3. Convert Memory to a strict RepositoryPort with typed retrieval methods (remove ad-hoc searches from kernel).
4. Narrow Executor to a pure ExecutorPort adapter that respects the incoming strategy and interaction_class without re-classifying or re-querying memory.
5. Keep Logic as the pure evaluation plugin (no changes needed yet beyond receiving Strategy).
These patterns together create the cognitive kernel you described: classification controls routing, retrieval is mandatory and early, executor is deterministic, memory is typed and auditable, and the system can evolve by adding plugins without losing clarity or boundaries.
The microkernel + hexagonal combination is the strongest match for Isaac’s stated principles and current structure. It turns the existing five modules into a stable, extensible foundation without requiring a full rewrite.


A3. GEMINI ANALYSIS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Hallo! Es ist eine spannende Aufgabe, die Architektur deines Projekts zu analysieren. Als dein Coding-Assistent konzentriere ich mich genau auf das, was du mir zur Verfügung gestellt hast – keine Spekulationen über den Rest des Codes und keine abstrakten Phrasen, sondern eine handfeste Analyse dieser fünf zentralen Kernmodule.
Hier ist die detaillierte architektonische Auswertung der ersten fünf Module (isaac_core.py, executor.py, low_complexity.py, logic.py, memory.py), genau nach deinen Vorgaben strukturiert.

1. CORE FLOW SUMMARY
Der aktuelle End-to-End-Ablauf zur Laufzeit (basierend auf isaac_core.process):
1. Eingang & Pre-Check: Der User-Input erreicht IsaacKernel.process(). Zuerst wird classify_interaction(low_complexity.py) aufgerufen. Wenn es sich um eine triviale Eingabe handelt (z. B. "Hallo", "Danke"), wird sofort eine statische lokale Antwort generiert und der Prozess beendet.
2. Intent-Erkennung: Wenn der Input komplexer ist, versucht detect_intent (isaac_core.py) per Regex das Ziel zu erraten (z. B. CODE, SEARCH, FILE). Es gibt eine Konfliktlösung (_resolve_intent_from_classification), die versucht, das Regex-Ergebnis mit der initialen Klassifikation abzugleichen.
3. Kontext-Retrieval: isaac_core.py ruft _retrieve_relevant_context auf. Dabei wird memory.py durchsucht (Fakten, Direktiven, Historie).
4. Strategie-Auswahl: Basierend auf Intent und Kontext wird eine Ausführungsstrategie festgelegt (Dürfen Tools genutzt werden? Ist Follow-up erlaubt?).
5. Routing: Der Kernel entscheidet, ob der Prompt an den Decomposer, einen Dispatcher (Multi-KI) oder als regulärer Task an den executor.py geht.
6. Ausführung (executor.py): Der Executor nimmt den Task an. Er entscheidet (teilweise selbstständig), ob Tools evaluiert werden (_maybe_use_tool). Er ruft das LLM auf (relay).
7. Evaluierung (logic.py): Die Antwort des LLMs wird an logic.py übergeben. Dort wird sie nach festen Kriterien (Wortanzahl, Floskeln, Themenabdeckung) bewertet.
8. Follow-Up-Loop: Wenn der Score zu niedrig ist, generiert logic.py einen Folge-Prompt. Der Executor loopt iterativ weiter, bis die Qualität ausreicht oder das Limit erreicht ist.
9. Speichern: Die finale Antwort geht zurück an isaac_core.py, wird durch Post-Processing (Regelwerk) ergänzt und über memory.py in der SQLite-Datenbank persistiert.

2. MODULE-BY-MODULE ANALYSIS
1. isaac_core.py (Kernel / Orchestrator)
• Current role: Haupteinstiegspunkt, Router und Daten-Aggregator.
• What it currently owns: Sudo-Verwaltung, Regex-basierte Intent-Erkennung, das Zusammenbauen des System-Prompts, High-Level-Routing und Zusammenführen des Kontextes.
• What it should own: Den übergeordneten Pipeline-Fluss. Er sollte entscheiden, in welcher Reihenfolge die Architekturschritte (Klassifikation -> Kontext -> Strategie -> Ausführung) durchlaufen werden.
• What it should NOT own: Hardcodierte Regex-Muster (PATTERNS). Das Formatieren von reinem Text für den Kontext (sollte ein eigener Prompt-Builder machen). Konfliktauflösung zwischen zwei verschiedenen Klassifikationssystemen.
• Relation to others: Importiert und kontrolliert alles. Es ist der zentrale Flaschenhals.
2. executor.py (Task Engine)
• Current role: Asynchrone Ausführungsmaschine, die LLM-Calls, Tool-Nutzung, Dateizugriffe und Code-Ausführung verwaltet.
• What it currently owns: Task-State-Management, Subprocess-Handling (Python-Code), Tool-Loop-Entscheidungen, Provider-Switching und die LLM-Nachfrage-Schleife.
• What it should own: Zuverlässige, deterministische Ausführung von strikt definierten Tasks (State-Management, Timeout, Error-Handling). Code in Sandboxen ausführen.
• What it should NOT own: Das Entscheiden, ob Tools bei einer bestimmten Interaktion genutzt werden sollen (das ist Strategie-Sache). Das Auslösen des Provider-Wechsels. Der Executor hat aktuell zu viel "eigenen Willen".
• Relation to others: Wird von isaac_core.py gefüttert, ruft logic.py zur Selbstkorrektur auf und schreibt am Ende in memory.py.
3. low_complexity.py (Fast-Path Classifier)
• Current role: Effizienter Filter für irrelevante oder hoch-triviale Inputs.
• What it currently owns: Keyword-Listen für Begrüßungen und statische Antwort-Generierung.
• What it should own: Schnelle, ressourcenschonende Klassifikation von Basis-Interaktionen, um Compute zu sparen.
• What it should NOT own: Komplexe LLM-basierte Intent-Erkennung.
• Relation to others: Wird ganz am Anfang von isaac_core.py aufgerufen, um einen Short-Circuit zu ermöglichen.
4. logic.py (Quality & Reflection Engine)
• Current role: Schiedsrichter über die Qualität der LLM-Antworten und Prompt-Generator für Korrekturen.
• What it currently owns: Regex-Listen für Floskeln, Wortzähl-Algorithmen und Themen-Extraktion zur Qualitätsbestimmung.
• What it should own: Die semantische und strukturelle Bewertung eines Ergebnisses anhand des definierten Ziels.
• What it should NOT own: Fest verdrahtete Schwellenwerte für reine Textlängen (Wortanzahl ist ein sehr fragiler Qualitätsindikator). Das direkte Formulieren der Follow-Up-Prompts (es sollte strukturierte Signale liefern, aus denen der Strategie-Layer Prompts baut).
• Relation to others: Agiert als reaktiver Dienstleister für den executor.py während der Ausführung.
5. memory.py (State & Memory Management)
• Current role: Die Langzeit-Persistenz-Schicht.
• What it currently owns: SQLite-Queries, Full-Text-Search (FTS), Vektor-Datenbank-Hooks und das Zusammenbauen von String-Blöcken für den Prompt (build_context).
• What it should own: Reine Datenspeicherung, relationale Verknüpfungen und effizientes Retrieval von typisierten Datenstrukturen.
• What it should NOT own: Die Methode build_context baut aktuell Strings (z.B. "[Relevante Fakten]\n...") direkt in der DB-Klasse. Das vermischt Datenhaltung mit Prompt-Engineering.
• Relation to others: Wird von isaac_core.py für den Pre-Flight-Kontext ausgelesen und vom executor.py nach Abschluss einer Aufgabe beschrieben.

3. CURRENT CONNECTION MAP
Wie die Module derzeit miteinander sprechen:
• Control Flow: isaac_core -> low_complexity (falls trivial -> Stop). Sonst: isaac_core -> executor. Der Executor geht in eine Schleife: executor -> logic -> executor. Nach Abschluss geht die Kontrolle zurück an isaac_core.
• Data Flow: Roher Text geht in isaac_core. isaac_core zieht Daten aus memory. Text + DB-Daten werden zu einem großen String vermengt und in den executor geworfen. Der Executor erzeugt eine Task-Instanz, reichert sie ggf. um Tool-Ergebnisse an und feuert sie ans LLM. Das Ergebnis wandert durch logic (was neue Text-Prompts generiert). Das fertige Datenpaket fließt am Ende in memory.
• Context Flow: Wird stark von memory.py und isaac_core.py geteilt. Beide bauen Teile des finalen Text-Kontexts zusammen, der dann starr in den Task gepresst wird.

4. MISSING OR WEAK LINKS
• Zwei konkurrierende Klassifikatoren: isaac_core.py hat detect_intent (Regex) und nutzt gleichzeitig classify_interaction aus low_complexity.py. Das führt zu Kollisionen (sichtbar in der Hilfsmethode _resolve_intent_from_classification).
• Vermischung von Daten und Präsentation: memory.py gibt fertige Prompt-Strings zurück statt sauberer Datenobjekte. Wenn die Strategie sich ändert, muss die Datenbank-Klasse angefasst werden.
• Der Executor ist zu "smart": Er entscheidet selbst in _should_try_tool, ob er Tools nutzt, basierend auf eigenen Hotwords (z. B. "wetter", "browser"). Das untergräbt das Architektonische Ziel, dass zuerst eine Strategie gewählt und dann stur ausgeführt wird.
• Die logic.py-Abhängigkeit von Wortanzahl: Die Qualität wird stark an der Länge gemessen (_score_length). Wenn das LLM die Antwort perfekt in 20 Wörtern zusammenfasst, triggert der Executor einen Follow-Up-Loop, weil logic.py unzufrieden ist. Das erzeugt unnötige Loops und Instabilität.
• Keine klare "Pre-Flight" vs "Execution"-Grenze: Kontext wird gesammelt, während sich Intents noch verschieben können. Die Tool-Strategie wird erst mitten im Task finalisiert.

5. TARGET CONNECTION MAP FOR THESE 5 MODULES
So sollten die 5 Module im Zielzustand verschaltet sein:
1. Classification (Einheitlich): low_complexity.py wird zu einem generellen Classifier-Modul ausgebaut (oder ersetzt die Regex-Patterns aus isaac_core). Jede Eingabe erhält eindeutige Typen (Trivial, Action, Inquiry, etc.).
2. Retrieval-before-response: isaac_core.py nutzt den klassifizierten Typ und fragt memory.py nach Rohdaten (Fakten, Direktiven, Historie). memory.py liefert nur Dictionaries/Objekte zurück.
3. Strategy Selection: isaac_core.py (oder ein neuer "Planner" innerhalb des Kernels) nimmt Typ + rohen Kontext und entscheidet strikt: Brauchen wir Tools? Sind Follow-Ups erlaubt? Welche Provider-Klasse?
4. Routing & Prompt-Building: Der Kernel baut den String-Prompt basierend auf der Strategie zusammen.
5. Executor Boundaries: Der executor.py bekommt einen absolut fertigen Arbeitsauftrag (Prompt + Erlaubte Tools + Strategie). Der Executor sucht nicht selbst nach Hotwords für Tools. Er arbeitet den Task ab.
6. Memory Usage: Nach Abschluss bewertet logic.py das Ergebnis. Das Resultat wird sauber strukturiert (nicht als riesiger Textblock) von isaac_core an memory.py zur Speicherung übergeben.

6. IMMEDIATE ARCHITECTURAL PRIORITIES
• Stabilisieren (First!): * Führe detect_intent aus dem Core und classify_interaction zusammen. Es darf nur eine "Source of Truth" für die Absicht des Users geben.
◦ Entferne das Prompt-Building (Strings zusammenkleben) aus memory.py. Lass es reine Daten (list[dict]) zurückgeben und baue den Text in isaac_core.py zusammen.
• Als Nächstes hinzufügen:
◦ Entziehe dem executor.py die autonome Tool-Entscheidung. Verlege diese Logik komplett in isaac_core._select_response_strategy, sodass der Executor nur noch Befehle ausführt.
• Noch NICHT bauen:
◦ Erweitere das Bewertungsmodell in logic.py noch nicht. Bevor das Metrik-System komplexer wird, muss die Endlosschleifen-Gefahr im Executor behoben werden (Weg von der Wortzähl-Metrik).
◦ Keine neuen Tool-Integrationen in den Executor einbauen, bis seine Grenzen strikt gezogen sind.

7. RISKS
Die größten Gefahren beim Weiterbauen auf dem aktuellen Stand:
1. Der "God-Executor"-Antipattern: Wenn du so weiter machst, wird executor.py zu einem unkontrollierbaren Monolithen, der entscheidet was getan wird, wie es getan wird und ob es gut war. Das bricht die "Executor must execute"-Prämisse.
2. Regex-Wartungshölle: Die harte Codierung von Wörtern wie "schreibe", "suche", "wetter" an drei verschiedenen Orten (isaac_core.py, executor.py, low_complexity.py) wird dazu führen, dass simple Befehle aus unerklärlichen Gründen falsch geroutet werden.
3. Infinite Follow-Up-Loops: Da logic.py "Kurze Antworten = Schlecht" bewertet, wird Isaac anfangen, bei simplen Nachfragen (Inquiry/Clarification) endlos zu loopen oder künstlich Text aufzublasen, nur um den Score des eigenen Kernels zu befriedigen.
Lass mich wissen, wie du diese Analyse findest und welchen architektonischen Schritt wir zuerst in Code gießen oder vertiefen sollen!


A4. DEEPSEEK ANALYSIS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Architecture Analysis – Isaac Core Modules (Package 1)

## 1. CORE FLOW SUMMARY

Based strictly on the 5 provided files, the current runtime flow is:

User Input → isaac_core.process()
    ↓
1. LOW_COMPLEXITY.classify_interaction()
    ↓ (if lightweight: immediate local response, bypass everything)
    ↓ (if normal: continue)
2. INTENT DETECTION (regex patterns inside isaac_core)
    ↓
3. SUDO CHECK + Empathy Analysis (internal to isaac_core)
    ↓
4. RETRIEVAL (two parallel, uncoupled retrievals):
    - memory.build_context() → general conversational + facts + directives
    - _retrieve_relevant_context() → preferences, project context, risks
    ↓
5. ROUTING DECISION (inside isaac_core._route):
    - Decomposer path (complex prompts)
    - Multi-KI path (explicit broadcast/split/pipeline)
    - Standard task path (default)
    ↓
6. EXECUTOR:
    - create_task() (stores task in _tasks dict)
    - submit_and_wait() (puts in PriorityQueue)
    - _execute() (dispatches to specific handler: _execute_ai, _execute_search, etc.)
    ↓
7. DURING EXECUTION (for AI tasks):
    - Optional: _maybe_use_tool() (calls external tool_runtime)
    - LOGIC.evaluate() (scores response)
    - LOGIC.decide_followup() (decides if another iteration needed)
    - Potentially provider switching
    ↓
8. MEMORY.add_conversation() (stores both sides)
    ↓
9. Return to user

**Critical observation:** Classification from low_complexity.py only gates *lightweight local responses*. For normal flows, it is **not** used to control routing, strategy selection, or tool access. Intent detection (regex in isaac_core) overrides classification in several places (`_resolve_intent_from_classification` overrides TOOL_REQUEST to SEARCH, overrides STATUS_QUERY to STATUS, but otherwise ignores classification for routing).

## 2. MODULE-BY-MODULE ANALYSIS

### 2.1 `low_complexity.py`

**Current role:** Early gating and local response generation.

**What it currently owns:**
- Interaction classification logic (greeting, acknowledgment, clarification, status, tool request, normal chat)
- Local response templates for lightweight classes
- Normalization utilities

**What it should own (target):**
- **Primary classification authority** for ALL inputs, not just lightweight gating
- Classification schema that controls routing decisions downstream
- Confidence scores for classification
- Clear mapping from classification → allowed response strategies

**What it should NOT own:**
- Response generation for anything beyond trivial social/acknowledgment (currently does this correctly)
- Intent detection (that's a different abstraction layer)
- Routing decisions (should output classification, not control flow)

**How it relates to others:**
- Currently: Called first in `process()`, then mostly ignored for normal flows
- Should: Feed classification to routing logic in isaac_core as primary control signal

### 2.2 `logic.py`

**Current role:** Quality evaluation + follow-up decision making.

**What it currently owns:**
- QualityScore (length, coverage, specificity, coherence metrics)
- FollowUpDecision (needed, mode, switch_provider, sub_tasks)
- TopicExtractor (domain detection from text)
- QualityEvaluator (scoring logic with evasion detection, multilingual)
- FollowUpGenerator (decision logic based on scores and topics)

**What it should own (target):**
- **Post-execution quality assessment** (current role is correct)
- **Iteration control** (whether to continue/stop/switch provider)
- **Decomposition suggestions** (identifying when a task needs splitting)

**What it should NOT own:**
- Response generation (doesn't)
- Tool invocation decisions (currently doesn't, but isaac_core uses its own `_should_try_tool` separately)
- Routing decisions (should receive context, not decide routing)

**How it relates to others:**
- Called by executor during `_execute_ai()` after each response
- Receives task.prompt and response, returns score
- Executor uses decision to either continue iteration or stop
- **Missing link:** logic doesn't receive classification from low_complexity, so follow-up decisions don't account for interaction type

### 2.3 `memory.py`

**Current role:** Persistent storage with SQLite + FTS, plus working memory cache.

**What it currently owns:**
- Conversation storage (add_conversation, get_working_memory, search_conversations)
- Fact storage (set_fact, get_fact, search_facts, all_facts)
- Directive storage (save_directive, revoke_directive, get_directives)
- Task result storage (save_task_result, get_relevant_results)
- Context building (build_context - mixes directives, facts, history, vector memory)
- Vector memory integration (delegates to vector_memory module)

**What it should own (target):**
- **All persistent state** (current role is correct)
- **Typed memory interfaces** (separate stores for different memory types with clear schemas)
- **Retrieval strategies** (semantic, keyword, temporal, relevance-ranked)

**What it should NOT own:**
- Response strategy logic (currently doesn't, but build_context is called unconditionally)
- Routing decisions
- Classification

**How it relates to others:**
- Called by isaac_core for:
  - build_context() before task creation
  - _retrieve_relevant_context() (separate method that queries memory again)
  - add_conversation() after response
  - save_task_result() after completion
- Called by executor? No direct calls in provided code (executor uses separate task persistence)
- **Problem:** Two parallel retrieval paths in isaac_core (build_context + _retrieve_relevant_context) that are not coordinated

### 2.4 `executor.py`

**Current role:** Task execution engine with async queue, persistence, tool integration, multi-provider relay.

**What it currently owns:**
- Task lifecycle (creation, queueing, execution, status tracking)
- Task persistence (SQLite via memory.save_task_result)
- Code execution (isolated subprocess)
- File operations (workspace-restricted)
- Search execution (via search module)
- Multi-KI execution (via dispatcher)
- AI task execution with iteration loop (tool use, scoring, follow-up)
- Tool state tracking (via task_tool_state module)
- Watchdog integration (progress recording)

**What it should own (target):**
- **Deterministic execution** of tasks (current role is correct)
- **Strict separation** between "what to execute" (task spec) and "how to execute" (executor internals)
- **Resource management** (concurrency, timeouts, limits)

**What it should NOT own:**
- **Intent interpretation** (currently receives prompt but doesn't reinterpret - good)
- **Response quality decisions** (calls logic for this - correct)
- **Routing decisions** (receives TaskType from caller - correct)
- **Memory storage decisions** (currently persists to memory - should remain, but memory should be caller's responsibility)

**Critical issue:** Executor has its own `_should_try_tool()` method that uses:
- task.allow_tools flag
- task.typ (excludes FILE, BROADCAST, SPLIT, PIPELINE)
- interaction_class (from task, but only checks TOOL_REQUEST)
- Hotword detection in prompt

This means **tool invocation logic is split** between:
- isaac_core: sets allow_tools flag based on _select_response_strategy
- executor: decides again based on hotwords and iteration

**How it relates to others:**
- Receives Task from isaac_core (via create_task + submit)
- Calls logic.evaluate() and logic.decide_followup()
- Calls memory.save_task_result() (but only DONE/FAILED tasks)
- Calls tool_runtime (external module)
- **Missing link:** Does NOT receive classification from low_complexity except via task.interaction_class

### 2.5 `isaac_core.py`

**Current role:** Orchestrator, routing, intent detection, SUDO, handler dispatch.

**What it currently owns:**
- Intent detection (regex patterns - 28 lines of pattern definitions)
- Routing decision (_route: decomposer vs multi-KI vs standard)
- Response strategy selection (_select_response_strategy - preferences, risks, style notes)
- Context retrieval (_retrieve_relevant_context - preferences, project context, behavioral risks)
- SUDO management
- Empathy integration
- Directive/fact handlers
- System prompt construction
- Memory context building (calls memory.build_context + formats its own retrieval)

**What it should own (target):**
- **Orchestration only** - coordinating between classification, retrieval, strategy, executor, memory
- **Session/context management** - maintaining user state across interactions
- **Policy enforcement** (SUDO, directives, gate)

**What it should NOT own:**
- **Classification logic** (should delegate entirely to low_complexity)
- **Intent detection as separate from classification** (should have unified classification that includes intent as a dimension)
- **Retrieval implementation** (should call typed memory interfaces, not implement its own `_retrieve_relevant_context` mixing memory queries with ad-hoc logic)
- **Strategy selection heuristics** (should be a separate module or at least clearly separated from orchestration)

**Critical issues:**
1. `_resolve_intent_from_classification` overrides classification but doesn't use it for routing in normal cases
2. `_retrieve_relevant_context` duplicates retrieval logic that partially overlaps with memory.build_context()
3. `_select_response_strategy` mixes preferences from memory with hardcoded rules about tool access
4. The `_route` method decides between decomposer/multi-KI/standard based on:
   - Intent (from regex)
   - Active instances
   - braucht_decomposer (word count + "und" count)
   - Sudo status

   **Classification from low_complexity is NOT used here** except for lightweight early exit.

## 3. CURRENT CONNECTION MAP

### Control Flow
User Input
    ↓
low_complexity.classify_interaction() → [lightweight? → local response, return]
    ↓
isaac_core.detect_intent() (regex)
    ↓
isaac_core._route()
    ├─→ decomposer (if complex and instances exist)
    ├─→ multi-KI (if explicit intent)
    └─→ standard → executor.create_task() → executor.submit_and_wait()
                        ↓
                   executor._execute()
                        ├─→ logic.evaluate()
                        ├─→ logic.decide_followup()
                        └─→ (iteration loop)
    ↓
memory.add_conversation() (steffen + isaac)
memory.save_task_result()
    ↓
return to user

### Data Flow
- **Prompt text** flows from user → isaac_core → executor → relay
- **Classification** flows from low_complexity → isaac_core (early exit only, then discarded)
- **Intent** flows from isaac_core patterns → routing decision → TaskType
- **Memory context** flows: memory.build_context() → isaac_core → executor.task.prompt (prepended)
- **Quality scores** flow: logic.evaluate() → executor.task.score → isaac_core (for post-processing)
- **Follow-up decisions** flow: logic.decide_followup() → executor (iteration control)

### Context Flow (where context is built, transformed, passed)
1. **Pre-execution context building** (isaac_core):
   - memory.build_context() → general context string
   - _retrieve_relevant_context() → structured dict (preferences, risks, reflections)
   - _format_retrieval_context() → string from that dict
   - Combined and prepended to user prompt

2. **During execution context** (executor):
   - tool_context_block() appended to prompt when tools used
   - No visibility into isaac_core's retrieval context

3. **No feedback loop** from executor outcomes back to retrieval strategy

## 4. MISSING OR WEAK LINKS

### 4.1 Classification → Routing (MISSING)
- Classification from low_complexity is **only used for early exit**
- Normal chat, tool request, ambiguous short all go through same routing logic
- `_resolve_intent_from_classification` overrides TOOL_REQUEST to SEARCH, but doesn't use classification for anything else
- **Result:** System treats "What's the weather?" (tool request) and "Tell me about Roman history" (normal chat) identically until executor's `_should_try_tool` checks hotwords

### 4.2 Retrieval → Strategy (WEAK)
- Two parallel retrieval mechanisms with overlapping concerns:
  - `memory.build_context()`: general directives + facts + history + vector
  - `_retrieve_relevant_context()`: preferences + project context + risks + reflections
- No coordination between them
- No feedback from executor outcomes to improve retrieval

### 4.3 Strategy Selection → Executor (IMPLICIT)
- `_select_response_strategy` returns flags (allow_tools, allow_followup, allow_provider_switch, style_note)
- These flags are passed to executor.create_task()
- **But:** executor has its own `_should_try_tool()` that re-evaluates tool necessity based on hotwords and iteration
- **Result:** Strategy says "no tools" but executor may still use tools if hotwords present

### 4.4 Logic → Executor (ASYMMETRIC)
- Logic evaluates quality and decides follow-up
- Executor implements the follow-up loop
- **But:** Logic has no visibility into:
  - Whether tools were used (affects quality expectations)
  - Which provider was used (affects switch decisions beyond provider ranking)
  - Task type (Chat vs Analysis vs Search have different quality expectations)

### 4.5 Memory → Classification (MISSING)
- Classification doesn't use historical patterns
- No way to learn that "short ambiguous inputs" from this user are usually clarification requests vs new topics
- No personalization of classification thresholds

### 4.6 Task Persistence (DUPLICATED)
- Executor has `_persist_task()` → calls memory.save_task_result()
- Memory has its own `save_task_result()`
- Executor also loads persisted tasks via `_load_persisted_tasks()` for dashboard only
- **No clear ownership:** Should executor persist, or should caller (isaac_core) persist after receiving result?

## 5. TARGET CONNECTION MAP FOR THESE 5 MODULES

### Desired Flow (Strict Separation of Concerns)

1. CLASSIFICATION (low_complexity)
   Input: raw user text
   Output: ClassificationResult {
       class: InteractionClass,
       confidence: float,
       sub_type: Optional[str],
       requires_response: bool,
       suggested_strategy: Optional[str]
   }
   ↓

2. RETRIEVAL (memory, typed interfaces)
   Input: ClassificationResult + user text
   Output: RetrievalContext {
       facts: List[Fact],
       history: List[ConversationTurn],
       directives: List[Directive],
       relevant_tasks: List[TaskResult],
       behavioral_patterns: List[Pattern]
   }
   ↓

3. STRATEGY SELECTION (new module or isaac_core with clear separation)
   Input: ClassificationResult + RetrievalContext + User text
   Output: ResponseStrategy {
       mode: "local" | "task" | "decompose" | "multi_ki",
       task_type: TaskType,
       allow_tools: bool,
       allow_followup: bool,
       allow_provider_switch: bool,
       system_prompt_modifiers: List[str],
       timeout_seconds: float
   }
   ↓

4. EXECUTION (executor)
   Input: Task (created from ResponseStrategy)
   Output: TaskResult {
       answer: str,
       score: QualityScore,
       provider_used: str,
       iterations: int,
       tools_used: List[ToolUse]
   }
   ↓

5. EVALUATION & MEMORY UPDATE (logic + memory)
   Input: TaskResult + Original context
   Actions:
     - logic.evaluate() already happened in executor
     - memory.add_conversation() for both sides
     - memory.save_task_result()
     - memory.update_facts() if new information
     - memory.record_outcome() for learning

### Key Changes Needed

**Classification becomes primary control:**
- `low_complexity.classify()` called ONCE per input
- Result passed to all downstream decisions
- No separate `detect_intent()` in isaac_core (or intent becomes a field in ClassificationResult)

**Retrieval unified:**
- Single `memory.retrieve()` method that takes classification + query
- Returns typed, structured context (not just string concatenation)
- isaac_core's `_retrieve_relevant_context` merged into memory

**Strategy selection explicit:**
- Separate function/module that outputs a Strategy object
- No hardcoded routing inside isaac_core._route
- Strategy includes tool policy, follow-up policy, provider policy

**Executor strictly executes:**
- No re-interpretation of strategy flags
- `_should_try_tool` removed; executor checks task.allow_tools only
- Tool invocation becomes part of strategy, not hotword detection

**Memory typed and queryable:**
- Separate stores for different memory types
- Retrieval methods return typed objects, not strings
- No `build_context()` that returns a string; instead caller formats retrieved objects

## 6. IMMEDIATE ARCHITECTURAL PRIORITIES

### Stabilize First (within these 5 modules)

1. **Classification as routing authority**
   - Modify `low_complexity.classify_interaction()` to return structured object with confidence
   - Remove `_resolve_intent_from_classification` override logic
   - Use classification in `_route` instead of `braucht_decomposer` heuristics

2. **Unify retrieval**
   - Merge `_retrieve_relevant_context` into `memory.retrieve_for_query()`
   - Remove duplicate retrieval code from isaac_core
   - Make retrieval return typed objects, not strings

3. **Remove executor tool re-decision**
   - Delete `_should_try_tool` from executor
   - Make `task.allow_tools` the ONLY control for tool invocation
   - Move hotword-based tool triggering to strategy selection (isaac_core)

### Add Next

4. **Explicit Strategy module**
   - Create `strategy.py` (not requested yet, but needed between current modules)
   - Move `_select_response_strategy` logic into it
   - Strategy receives Classification + RetrievalContext, outputs ResponseStrategy

5. **Feedback from executor to memory**
   - After task completion, memory should receive outcome quality
   - Memory updates retrieval weights based on success/failure
   - Currently no learning mechanism in these 5 modules

### Do NOT Build Yet

- Full vector memory integration (low_complexity doesn't need it)
- Complex tool orchestration (executor's tool state is sufficient for now)
- Multi-agent coordination beyond current dispatcher calls
- Real-time learning loops (stabilize retrieval first)

## 7. RISKS

### Most Dangerous Architectural Mistakes if Continuing Without Fixes

1. **Classification atrophy** – If low_complexity remains only an early-exit gate, the system will never develop differentiated behavior for "greeting" vs "clarification" vs "tool request" vs "complex analysis". All normal inputs will follow the same heavy orchestration path, wasting resources and muddying behavior.

2. **Retrieval fragmentation** – Two parallel retrieval paths (`build_context` + `_retrieve_relevant_context`) will diverge over time. One will be optimized for conversation, one for preferences, but neither will be comprehensive. Result: inconsistent context quality and hard-to-debug behavior.

3. **Tool invocation split-brain** – Executor's `_should_try_tool` will continue to override strategy flags. When a user explicitly says "no tools" but uses hotwords, executor will use tools anyway. This violates user expectations and makes policy enforcement impossible.

4. **Memory as string blender** – `memory.build_context()` returning a string encourages downstream modules to treat memory as opaque. No structured access means no ability to selectively retrieve or update. The system will accumulate context bloat without ability to prune or prioritize.

5. **No learning loop** – Currently, executor → logic → memory is one-way. Executor doesn't tell memory whether the retrieved context was useful. Memory doesn't update retrieval weights. The system will never improve its own context selection.

6. **Executor responsibility creep** – Executor already contains search, file, code, multi-KI, and AI task execution. Adding more specialized handlers will make it a monolith. The boundary should be: executor executes Tasks, Task types determine handler, but Task creation and routing belong elsewhere.

7. **Isaac_core as god object** – Already contains: intent detection, routing, strategy, SUDO, empathy, directive handling, fact handling, status, context building, system prompt construction. This will become impossible to test or modify. Each of these should be separate modules or at least separate classes with clear interfaces.

**Immediate highest risk:** Continuing to build on current structure without fixing the classification → routing link. Every new feature will either duplicate routing logic or introduce inconsistencies. Fix classification authority FIRST, then retrieval, then executor boundaries.



====================================================================================================
B. ISAAC TARGET ARCHITECTURE / CONCEPT / EVOLUTION 2.0
====================================================================================================

ISAAC TARGET STATE, CONCEPT, AND EVOLUTION 2.0

Purpose
Isaac is not intended to remain a generic chatbot, a simple tool router, or only a UI over models. Isaac is intended to become a local, stateful, evolving AI system — a cognitive kernel.

Core mission
Isaac should:
1. receive user input
2. classify the interaction correctly
3. retrieve relevant past context BEFORE deciding how to respond
4. select a response strategy
5. execute the chosen path deterministically
6. evaluate outcomes
7. store structured memory
8. learn gradually from interactions, feedback, repeated mistakes, and explicit guidance
9. evolve over time without losing type separation, architectural clarity, constitutional boundaries, or identity stability

Isaac is not supposed to be a static system. Isaac should develop through:
- remembered interactions
- stored preferences
- project-specific context
- behavioral corrections
- reflected knowledge about what worked and failed
- explicit user guidance

But Isaac must remain:
- explainable
- deterministic enough to debug
- architecturally disciplined
- auditable
- bounded in its self-change

High-level architecture target
The intended end-state is a layered system with these major functions:
- interaction classification
- deterministic routing
- retrieval-before-response
- typed memory
- structured interaction logging
- inquiry / clarification logic
- gradual feedback-driven learning
- bounded behavioral development / “character” evolution
- explicit separation between facts, preferences, hypotheses, reflections, project context, interaction records, and behavior patterns

Core architectural principles
1. Classification must control routing.
2. Retrieval must happen before response strategy selection.
3. Executor must execute, not silently redefine intent.
4. Memory must be typed, not a single undifferentiated store.
5. Inquiry / clarification must be deterministic and justified, not random.
6. Learning must be gradual, evidence-based, and auditable.
7. Core identity / constitution rules must not drift implicitly.
8. Trivial social inputs must never trigger unnecessary heavy orchestration.
9. Normal chat must not opportunistically trigger tools.
10. Status queries, tool requests, normal chat, and social lightweight inputs must remain distinct.

Isaac should ultimately behave like this
When the user sends an input, Isaac should:
- understand what kind of interaction it is
- decide what degree of complexity is needed
- load only the relevant past context
- pick the safest and most useful response strategy
- execute without hidden re-interpretation inside lower layers
- log what happened
- learn carefully from the result

In concrete terms:
- Greetings should be local, cheap, and quiet.
- Clarifications should be short and not explode into loops.
- Normal chat should stay normal chat and not opportunistically tool.
- Status should stay status.
- Explicit search/tool requests should stay tool-oriented.
- Ambiguous short inputs should not be forced into lightweight-local by default.
- Follow-up should happen only when justified, not because of noisy executor heuristics.

What “Evolution 2.0” means in this project
Evolution 2.0 is not “Isaac randomly changes personality.”
Evolution 2.0 means:
- Isaac becomes more accurate in routing.
- Isaac remembers more relevant user-specific guidance.
- Isaac stops repeating avoidable mistakes.
- Isaac asks better, fewer, and more justified questions over time.
- Isaac improves its response strategy from experience.
- Isaac develops a more coherent working style without drifting away from its core constraints.

Evolution 2.0 includes five major long-term layers:
1. Retrieval-before-response becomes mandatory and reliable.
2. Typed memory becomes the basis for selective context activation.
3. Interaction and outcome logging become structured and complete.
4. Inquiry / education-phase logic becomes deterministic and contextual.
5. Feedback and learning update strategy and policy gradually.

What Isaac should not become
- not a giant uncontrolled agent framework
- not a random personality simulator
- not a pure prompt spaghetti system
- not a model-specific wrapper whose behavior changes unpredictably with provider switches
- not a memory dump machine that injects everything everywhere
- not a system where executor decides strategy on its own

Typed memory model
Isaac should eventually separate at least these memory types:
- interaction_records
- facts
- preferences
- project_context
- behavior_patterns
- hypotheses
- reflections
- feedback_signals
- possibly trust-related data later

These must not be mixed casually.
A fact is not a preference.
A hypothesis is not a fact.
A reflection is not a directive.
A past interaction is not automatically a stable behavior pattern.

Retrieval-before-response
This is mandatory in the intended architecture.
Isaac must not answer first and remember later.
Instead:
- input is classified
- relevant context is retrieved
- strategy is chosen using that retrieved context
- only then is the task executed

The retrieval should be selective, not a giant memory dump.
It should prefer:
- user preferences relevant to the current request
- project context relevant to the current task
- prior similar interactions if they matter
- known behavioral risks or previous mistakes
- relevant reflections if they are useful to the current decision

Inquiry / education phase
Isaac should eventually include an Inquiry Layer.
This is not for random questioning.
It is for deterministic, justified learning-oriented follow-up.
It should only ask when at least one of the following is true:
- intent or classification uncertainty matters
- a required parameter is missing
- a new memory-worthy preference or rule is detected
- a similar previous mistake suggests asking first would prevent failure
- a relevant preference gap exists

It must NOT ask in these cases:
- social greeting
- social acknowledgment
- short clarification
- clear status queries
- clear tool requests with sufficient parameters

Inquiry should be logged with reasons.
It should support the education phase by helping Isaac learn better rules and preferences, then naturally asking less over time because the needed context is already known.

Learning model
Learning should be gradual.
No one-shot self-rewrites.
No sudden personality pivots.
No random curiosity values.

Learning should happen through:
- repeated interaction outcomes
- explicit feedback
- implicit correction signals
- detected patterns of success and failure
- controlled policy adjustment

What may be adjusted slowly:
- response style preferences
- clarification thresholds
- tool caution
- brevity vs depth
- error prevention heuristics

What must not mutate freely:
- constitutional limits
- safety boundaries
- type separation rules
- core identity constraints

Constitution / identity boundary
Isaac should eventually have a bounded development model.
That means it can become:
- more useful
- more aligned with the owner’s working style
- more stable and precise
- less repetitive in mistakes

But it must not become:
- implicitly self-rewriting
- silently manipulative
- structurally chaotic
- indistinguishable from a different system after enough iterations

Current implementation order agreed in this chat
1. Stabilize routing and runtime behavior.
2. Enforce retrieval-before-response in a clearer and more structured way.
3. Build typed memory / typed retrieval.
4. Add structured interaction and outcome logging.
5. Add deterministic inquiry / education-phase follow-up.
6. Add feedback-driven learning.
7. Postpone full trust logic until the core runs stably.

Trust in the current phase
Trust is intentionally NOT a current implementation priority.
Because there is effectively one primary owner/user context right now, trust can stay at a constant high default in early phases. Dynamic trust should only be added later, after routing, retrieval, memory, inquiry, and learning are stable.

Instruction for external models / coding agents
When analyzing or implementing Isaac:
- there is no time limit
- prioritize correctness, structure, and architectural coherence over speed
- do not optimize for novelty
- do not produce generic chatbot advice
- do not redesign the entire system unless explicitly requested
- do not add giant subsystems prematurely
- do not ignore the intended end-state described above
- architecture and planning remain under human control
- implementation agents should operate on scoped work packages, not open-ended rewrites
